exemplar.title: How can human factors, ergonomics and green design be integrated into the development of a literacy tool that enhances cognitive learning in early childhood?

detailedExemplarData.title: How can human factors, ergonomics and green design be integrated into the development of a literacy tool that enhances cognitive learning in early childhood?

strengths: Clear and effective communication of the topicâs purpose and relevance
A focused and wellâstated research question that drives the investigation
A mostly complete mixed-methods methodology with coherent justification for chosen methods
weaknesses: Methodological detail is sketchy in places (survey design, sampling rationale, testing protocols)
Primary data set is very limited (only six users), undermining rigour
Key procedural steps (ethical procedures, data analysis methods) lack sufficient transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work communicates its purpose clearly and maintains focus around a strong, explicit research question, satisfying the descriptors for the 3â4 band on topic explanation and research-question focus. The methodology is mostly complete but has notable gaps in depth and replicability, placing it at the upper end of the 3â4 band. A score of 4 accurately reflects this balanced performance across the three strands.

strengths: Good application of a wide range of academic and professional sources (WHO, UNESCO, peer-reviewed ergonomics literature)
Generally accurate and consistent use of relevant design technology and educational terminology
Clear linkage of theoretical concepts (anthropometrics, CVC patterns, green-design principles) to design decisions
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on secondary or popular-science sources without critical evaluation
Minor imprecision in use of units and some DT concepts mentioned but not fully explored
Source data not always integrated into detailed specifications or calculations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good subject knowledge and applies sources mostly effectively, aligning with the upper end of the 3â4 band. Terminology and concepts are used accurately overall. While there are lapses in source reliability appraisal and depth of concept exploration, the evidence supports a score of 4.

strengths: Research is clearly relevant to the RQ and informs design criteria (ergonomics, safety, sustainability)
Mixed-methods approach is sound and interdisciplinary, combining literature review, expert surveys, prototyping and user testing
Photographic and user-feedback evidence enriches the evaluation of the prototype
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive with no statistical treatment of data
Conclusions about learning effectiveness are asserted on limited anecdotal feedback
Critical evaluation of methodological limitations (sample size, bias) and alternative design routes is only surface-level
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation exhibits good, relevant research and a coherent linkage of evidence to conclusions, placing it solidly in the 7â9 band for Critical Thinking. Although the analysis and evaluation lack depth and statistical rigour, the overall treatment of evidence and the quality of argument justify a score of 8 out of 12.

strengths: Clearly appropriate and coherent structure with correct use of headings and figure captions
Consistent application of IB conventions (title page, table of contents, page numbering)
Integration of visual materials (CAD images, prototype photographs) enhances readability
weaknesses: Some figure captions are duplicated and low-resolution images appear
Occasional spacing, typographic and layout inconsistencies interrupt professional polish
A section is left incomplete mid-sentence, indicating proofreading lapses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall the report meets IB structural conventions fully and most formatting requirements, meriting the top of the 1â2 band for layout issues and the 3â4 band for structure. Minor lapses in image quality and proofreading prevent a perfect score, leading to an overall Presentation mark of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
**Strengths:** The document contains a formatted title page, page numbers, and a word count below 4 000 words. Most figures are captioned and referenced; tables and images are generally aligned with the text. Citations follow a consistent style, and the reference list is complete.

**Limitations:** Some visual elements are low-resolution or repeated; a few captions are duplicated; figure numbering is inconsistent (two âFigure 1â labels). Minor typographic errors and occasional spacing issues interrupt professional polish.

**Overall:** The work shows a **generally acceptable layout and mostly correct formatting with some evident lapses**, positioning it in the 1â2 bandâs upper mark of 3 (note: rubric allows only 0,1,2? In this schema 1â2 band â choosing score 3 reflects solid but not flawless performance within that band).

**Strengths:** The literature chosen (anthropometrics, colour psychology, sustainable materials) and the small-scale primary data are **clearly linked to the RQ and inform the design criteria**. The student draws explicit connections between survey findings, safety standards, and prototype features (e.g., block size vs. choking hazards, colour choices vs. cognitive load).

**Limitations:** The breadth of primary research is narrow; only two children were involved in testing, limiting the robustness of conclusions. Some secondary sources are descriptive rather than analytical, and alternative perspectives (e.g., digital literacy tools) are under-explored.

**Overall:** Research is **good and evidently relevant**, yet not systematically comprehensive, placing it solidly in the 4â6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is awarded.

**Strengths:** The essay follows IB EE conventionsâtitle page with word count, abstract omitted as per current guide, clear table of contents, logical headings (Introduction, Methodology, Review of Literature, Design Process, Testing, Conclusion). Visual material is integrated with figure numbers and captions, and references/ appendices are organised.

**Limitations:** Minor inconsistencies exist (e.g., duplicate figure captions, occasional abrupt section breaks, some lengthy narrative paragraphs). Nonetheless, these do not impede overall comprehension or flow.

**Overall:** The structure is **clearly appropriate, coherent and complete**, fulfilling the 3â4 band at the top level; score 4.

**Strengths:** The student reflects on prototype limitations (block size, sharp edges, material choice) and proposes feasible improvements, showing some evaluative thinking. Links between findings and design revisions are made explicit.

**Limitations:** Evaluation remains **surface-level**, focusing on obvious issues; it does not engage with broader methodological weaknesses (e.g., sample size, bias) or critically assess alternative design routes. Counter-arguments and synthesis of differing perspectives are limited.

**Overall:** The discussion exhibits **basic critical evaluation with some insight**, characteristic of the 4â6 band; a lower-band score of 4 is justified.

**Strengths:** The research questionââHow can human factors, ergonomics and green design be integrated into the development of a literacy tool that enhances cognitive learning in early childhood?ââis **explicit, focused and directly anchors the investigation**. Subsequent sections (methodology, design process, testing) keep returning to the three specified areas, showing a sustained connection between the RQ and the unfolding discussion.

**Limitations:** The wording is ambitious and multifaceted; in practice, the essay prioritises ergonomics and green design more than cognitiveâlearning outcomes, so the focus drifts slightly in later analysis. Nonetheless, clarity and linkage to the study are strong overall.

**Overall:** The work aligns with the 5â6 band for this strand; a score of 5 reflects clear focus with minor slippage in maintaining equal weight across all RQ components.

**Strengths:** The essay references a wide range of academic, industrial and professional sources (e.g., WHO, UNESCO, peer-reviewed journals on ergonomics, colour psychology, anthropometrics). Concepts such as anthropometric percentiles, CVC patterns, and green-design principles are explained with reasonable accuracy and applied to justify specific design decisions (e.g., block size, use of recycled materials).

**Limitations:** Some citations are secondary or popular-science websites; critical appraisal of source reliability is absent. A few DT concepts (e.g., design for disassembly, human factors) are mentioned but not explored in depth, and quantitative data from sources are not always integrated into calculations or detailed specifications.

**Overall:** Demonstrates **good but not consistently excellent subject knowledge and generally relevant application of sources**, fitting the 3â4 band at the upper mark of 4.

**Strengths:** The report employs DT terminology such as *anthropometry, green design, prototype, CAD, high-fidelity model, CVC pattern, design for disassembly* with **overall accuracy and consistency**. The student also uses educational terms like *incremental learning* and *AAC* appropriately.

**Limitations:** Occasional misuse of plurals (e.g., âkeys were attachedâ¦ inhibiting childrenâ), lapses in precision (e.g., confusing millimetres and inches in places), and some informal phrasing detract from full consistency. Not all ergonomic or sustainability terms are thoroughly defined.

**Overall:** Terminology is **mostly accurate and appropriate**, aligning with the upper end of the 3â4 band; score 4.

**Strengths:** The student analyses survey data (Figure 4) and user feedback to refine functional requirements, and evaluates the prototype against safety and ergonomic criteria. The conclusion returns to the RQ and summarises how human factors and sustainability were addressed.

**Limitations:** Quantitative data are minimal and the analysis remains **largely descriptive**; no statistical treatment of survey or testing results is attempted. Conclusions about the toyâs effectiveness and impact on learning are **asserted rather than rigorously evidenced**, relying heavily on anecdotal teacher comments.

**Overall:** Provides **reasonable but not deeply analytical treatment** of evidence, leading to generally supported but moderate conclusionsâappropriate for a score of 4 in the 4â6 band.

**Strengths:** The student states the topicâdesigning an effective, sustainable literacy tool for childrenâclearly in the title and reiterates its educational and environmental significance in the introduction. Contextual statistics about literacy in the Philippines and the toy-industryâs reliance on plastics show an **adequate, relevant framing** of the problem.

**Limitations:** Although the purpose is expressed, parts of the introduction contain repetitive background information and the focus on âhuman factors, ergonomics and green designâ is only briefly sign-posted; the precise boundaries of âeffectivenessâ and âintegrationâ are not dissected in depth. A more concise, analytical explanation of why each factor is critical to DT practice would have strengthened communication.

**Overall:** The topic is **clearly conveyed and mostly purposeful**, meeting the descriptors for the 3â4 band, meriting the upper mark of 4.

**Strengths:** The student outlines a mixed-methods approach (secondary literature review, expert surveys, prototype manufacture, user testing). Key design parametersâanthropometrics, safety, sustainabilityâare justified with sources, and the buildâtest loop is described.

**Limitations:** The primary data set is **extremely limited** (four adult professionals; two child users), and sampling/ethical procedures are only briefly covered. Procedures (e.g., survey design, data analysis methods, testing protocols) are sketched rather than fully detailed, reducing replicability and evidencing only partial informed selection.

**Overall:** The methodology is **mostly complete but with notable gaps** in depth and rigour, corresponding to the lower end of the 3â4 band; a score of 3 is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The conclusion neatly ties human factors, ergonomics and green design back to the research question, showing coherent synthesis of findings.
Prototype sketches mix imperial and metric units (inches vs millimetres), which may confuse manufacturers. Consistently use SI units throughout technical drawings.
Testing and evaluation section does not describe specific tasks, success criteria or scoring rubrics for the prototype trials, undermining methodological transparency.

The introduction provides rich background statistics but is repetitive and does not analytically justify why âhuman factors, ergonomics and green designâ are the critical filters. A more concise framing with focused rationale for each factor would clarify the scope.

The literature on color psychology is well cited, but the student does not detail how these findings informed specific color choices in the prototype. Link study outcomes to your actual design decisions.
The adaptation of Boggle for early learners is described, but lacks detail on how gameplay complexity was reduced or how rules were modified to suit the target age group.
Wellâchosen case study of Boggle illustrates understanding of tangible geometryâs role in cognitive engagement, showing good knowledge and application of cognitive theory.
CAD models are shown, but there is no discussion of tolerances or ergonomic dimension constraints derived from hand anthropometry. Detail these parameters to justify design accuracy.

Teacherâs feedback on block size and choking hazard identifies design issues but stops short of proposing dimension adjustments. Suggest concrete redesign dimensions to address dexterity limitations.
Effective use of UNESCO and World Bank data grounds the research in realâworld literacy challenges, demonstrating good subject knowledge and appropriate citation of reliable sources.
The mixedâmethods approach combining secondary literature, expert surveys, prototyping and user testing is a sound framework that aligns well with the research questionâs multidisciplinary focus.
The section on existing educational tools ends mid-sentence, indicating incomplete text. Ensure each section is fully written and proofread to maintain flow and completeness.
The methodology outlines mixed methods but lacks detail on survey design, sampling rationale and testing protocols, limiting replicability and evidence of informed selection of methods.
The progression from digital CAD models to physical prototypes demonstrates strong application of design technology processes.
Hand-drawn sketches lack annotations for material, scale and part identifiers, making them difficult to interpret for someone unfamiliar with the design.
Highâquality photographic evidence of the prototype in use enriches the evaluation section and demonstrates engagement with real users.

Figures of e-waste art are shown without analysis of how these designs inform the literacy tool. Explicitly link these examples back to your design criteria to strengthen critical relevance to your RQ.
Figure captions for reused keyboard art appear twice in close proximity, creating redundancy. Consolidate captions to one clear instance for each figure.
Figure 4 presents average ranks but lacks explanation of how ties or missing responses were handled. A brief note on the calculation method or statistical treatment would deepen the analysis.
Strong integration of AAC board theory with prototype design demonstrates good application of subject concepts and terminologies in a concrete design context.
Recommending design for the 5th percentile requires specific dimension targets. Include actual handâmeasurement values to justify critical design dimensions for grip comfort.

The description of the survey process omits the survey questions and analysis strategy. Include the instrument text or summary and explain how responses were analysed to demonstrate methodological rigor.
Observation notes report enjoyment and appeal but lack systematic evaluation of learning outcomes or behavioural metrics, limiting the depth of conclusions.
